title: Try it YourSelf - Forking a Repository

content.problemStatement:
**Step 1: Fork the repository** \
**Go to the repo**: clone-practice-repo \
(https://github.com/codechef8899/clone-practice-repo/) \
**Click Fork** → now the repo will appear in your GitHub account
- **Rename** it to: `fork-repo`

**Step 2: Clone your fork locally** \
Copy the HTTPS URL of your fork and run:
```sss
git clone 
```
This creates a local copy of your fork.

**Step 3: Go to your `fork-repo` folder and Create a new branch**
```SSS
cd fork-repo
git checkout -b feature-branch
```
This keeps your changes separate from the `main` branch

**Step 4: Create a new file in your `fork-repo` folder** \
Open `fork-repo` folder from your workspace then \
Create a file named `sample.txt` in it \
Add some text inside it.

**Step 5: Commit your changes**
```SSS
git add sample.txt
git commit -m "Add sample.txt file"
```
When Git asks for configuration during a commit, it wants two things: \
Username and Email : **These must match your GitHub account** (the one that owns the fork)

**Step 6: Push your branch to your fork** \
Before push you can check the Github Repo URL on which you want to push i.e (fork repo) \
Run the command :
```sss
git remote -v
```
Then Push your branch on the forked repo :
```SSS
git push -u origin feature-branch
```
This uploads your branch to your forked repo on GitHub

**Step 7: Verify** \
Go to your GitHub fork → check that `feature-branch` exists with the `sample.txt` file

---
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
